title: Ἀστρολόγος
source: Émile Chambry, Ésope: Fables, texte établi et traduit (Les Belles Lettres, Paris, 1927), fable 65 (= Perry 40). Text as printed, including Chambry's editorial punctuation (« », ⟨ ⟩ for editorial supplements). Pronunciation: reconstructed Attic, after W. S. Allen, Vox Graeca. Romanization: standard classical transliteration (ē/ō; th/ph/ch; y for υ, u in diphthongs; h = rough breathing; ēi/āi/ōi for iota subscript; accents omitted).

sentences.t: Ἀστρολόγος ἐξιὼν ἑκάστοτε ἑσπέρας ἔθος εἶχε τοὺς ἀστέρας ἐπισκοπῆσαι.
sentences.tr: Astrologos exiōn hekastote hesperas ethos eiche tous asteras episkopēsai.
sentences.l: A star-gazer, going-out each-time of-an-evening, had the-habit the stars to-survey.
sentences.i: An astronomer had the habit of going out every evening to study the stars.
sentences.n: ἀστρολόγος is simply 'star-studier' — astronomy and astrology were not yet distinct trades. ἑσπέρας: genitive of time, 'in the evening.' ἔθος εἶχε + infinitive: 'had the custom of.'

sentences.t: Καὶ δή ποτε περιιὼν εἰς τὸ προάστειον καὶ τὸν νοῦν ὅλον ἔχων πρὸς τὸν οὐρανὸν ἔλαθε καταπεσὼν εἰς φρέαρ.
sentences.tr: Kai dē pote periiōn eis to proasteion kai ton noun holon echōn pros ton ouranon elathe katapesōn eis phrear.
sentences.l: And so once, walking-about into the outskirts and the mind whole having toward the sky, he-escaped-notice having-fallen into a well.
sentences.i: One night, strolling in the outskirts with his whole mind on the sky, he fell into a well before he knew it.
sentences.n: ἔλαθε καταπεσών — λανθάνω + participle, one of Greek's neatest idioms: literally 'he escaped notice falling,' i.e. he fell without noticing/being noticed. The construction makes the obliviousness grammatical.

sentences.t: Ὀδυρομένου δὲ αὐτοῦ καὶ βοῶντος, παριών τις, ὡς ἤκουσε τῶν στεναγμῶν, προσελθὼν καὶ μαθὼν τὰ συμβεβηκότα, ἔφη πρὸς αὐτόν· « Ὦ οὗτος, σὺ τὰ ἐν οὐρανῷ βλέπειν πειρώμενος τὰ ἐπὶ τῆς γῆς οὐχ ὁρᾷς; »
sentences.tr: Odyromenou de autou kai boōntos, pariōn tis, hōs ēkouse tōn stenagmōn, proselthōn kai mathōn ta symbebēkota, ephē pros auton: « Ō houtos, sy ta en ouranōi blepein peirōmenos ta epi tēs gēs ouch horāis; »
sentences.l: And he wailing and shouting, a passer-by, when he-heard the groans, having-approached and having-learned the things-having-happened, said to him: « You there, you, the things in heaven to-see trying, the things on the earth not you-see? »
sentences.i: As he wailed and shouted, a passer-by heard the groaning, came up, and on learning what had happened said to him: « My friend — you strive to see what is in the heavens, and you cannot see what lies at your feet? »
sentences.n: The punchline balances two article-phrases: τὰ ἐν οὐρανῷ against τὰ ἐπὶ τῆς γῆς — 'the things in heaven' versus 'the things on earth.' Thales is told this same story with a servant-girl doing the laughing (Plato, Theaetetus 174a).

sentences.t: Τούτῳ τῷ λόγῳ χρήσαιτο ἄν τις ἐπ' ἐκείνων τῶν ἀνθρώπων οἳ παραδόξως ἀλαζονεύονται, μηδὲ τὰ κοινὰ τοῖς ἀνθρώποις ἐπιτελεῖν δυνάμενοι.
sentences.tr: Toutōi tōi logōi chrēsaito an tis ep' ekeinōn tōn anthrōpōn hoi paradoxōs alazoneuontai, mēde ta koina tois anthrōpois epitelein dynamenoi.
sentences.l: This story might-use someone against those men who extravagantly boast, not-even the common-things for-men to-accomplish being-able.
sentences.i: One might use this story against people who boast of wonders while unable to manage what any man can do.
sentences.n: A fourth moral formula: χρήσαιτο ἄν τις, 'one might apply (this story)…' — the potential optative with ἄν. ἀλαζονεύονται: the ἀλαζών, the pretentious boaster, is a stock character of Greek comedy and of these morals.
